lesson.menu_desc: Answering the audience with confidence -- indirect questions and clarifying -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 13 -- Handling Questions -- the Q&A after your talk
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Nilo Mesquita Patussi | Aula 13 -- Handling Questions

listenings.text: Hello Doctor Nilo, this is Sofia again from the FIFA Leadership in Football program office. Last week you gave your presentation, and it went very well. Today I want to prepare you for the hardest part: the question and answer session at the end. When you finish your talk and invite questions, the committee will challenge you, so here is some friendly advice. First, always listen to the whole question before you answer; never interrupt the person. Second, if a question is not clear, ask the speaker to rephrase it. You can say, sorry, could you rephrase that, or, in other words, are you asking about the budget? This buys you a few seconds to think. Third, be polite and indirect. Instead of a direct question like, what do you mean, say, could you tell me what you mean, or, may I ask why you think that. Indirect questions sound softer and more professional. Fourth, if you do not know an answer, do not guess. It is perfectly fine to say, that is a fair point, but I do not have that figure off hand. Let me get back to you by email after the meeting. Fifth, when a question is complex, break it down into smaller parts and address one part at a time. And finally, always thank the person for the question before you answer. Remember, Doctor, a good answer is calm, short, and honest. You do not need to know everything. You only need to handle each question with confidence. Good luck in the hot seat.

Let me tell you how the question and answer session went after my presentation to the compliance committee. When I finished, I said, thank you, I am happy to take any questions, and I will be honest, that moment always makes me a little nervous. The first question was clear, so I thanked the person and answered it in two sentences. The second question was long and had two parts, so I broke it down. I said, let me address your first point about the budget, and then I will come back to the timeline. That helped me stay organized. Then a committee member asked something I did not fully understand, so I did not pretend. I asked him to rephrase the question, and I said, in other words, are you asking about the new audit procedure? He said yes, and then I could answer well. The hardest moment came near the end. Someone asked for an exact figure from two years ago, and I did not have it off hand. Instead of guessing, I said, that is a fair point, but I do not have that number with me right now. Let me get back to you by email tomorrow. The committee respected that honest answer. To handle the questions politely, I used indirect forms all the time. Instead of saying, why do you think that, I said, may I ask why you think that. It sounded much softer. When the session ended, the chair thanked me, and for the first time I felt that I could handle a tough Q and A in English with confidence.
